name: Ploceidae
description: Weavers are a large family of passerine birds known for their elaborately woven nests. They are primarily found in sub-Saharan Africa, with a few species occurring in Asia, and are named for their remarkable nest-building behavior.

children.name: Ploceus
children.description: A large genus of weavers with stout conical bills, known for their bright breeding plumages and woven nests.

children.name: Ploceus cucullatus
children.description: The Village Weaver is a common bird with black head and yellow body in breeding males. It is found across sub-Saharan Africa, building large woven nests in colonies near water.

children.name: Ploceus philippinus
children.description: The Baya Weaver is a sparrow-sized bird with bright yellow crown and breast in breeding males. It is famous for its intricately woven retort-shaped nests, built in colonies across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Ploceus velatus
children.description: The Southern Masked Weaver has a black face and throat with bright yellow body in breeding males. It is common across southern Africa, weaving spherical nests in trees and reed beds.

children.name: Ploceus spekei
children.description: Speke's Weaver is a yellow weaver with a black face, throat, and bill in breeding males. It is endemic to the Horn of Africa, inhabiting savannas and building woven nests in acacia trees.

children.name: Quelea
children.description: A genus of small weavers that form some of the largest bird flocks on Earth.

children.name: Quelea quelea
children.description: The Red-billed Quelea is a small weaver with a distinctive red bill and variable breeding plumage. It forms enormous flocks that can number in the millions, and is considered a major agricultural pest in Africa.

children.name: Amblyospiza
children.description: A monotypic genus of large, thick-billed weaver found in African wetlands and forests.

children.name: Amblyospiza albifrons
children.description: The Grosbeak Weaver is a large, robust weaver with a massive conical bill and a white forehead patch. It inhabits reed beds and forest edges across sub-Saharan Africa, building large woven nests.
